--- SOURCE 2: Recent advances in microbial electrosynthesis system: Metabolic ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1369703X23001237

SUMMARY:
Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) presents a promising technology for bio-electrochemical conversion of released CO 2 and organic wastes into electricity and biofuel-chemicals using external-powered and/or self-powered microbial oxidation/reduction processes. The previously published review articles mainly summarized the concept of MES, biocathodic production processes, variety of substrates used, the architecture of electrode materials and bioreactor designs, microbial engineering and electron transfer pathways, potential synthesis of value-added products, state-of-art of the MES, and critical review on the recent development of the MES technology (Table 1). Therefore, to achieve the full potential and successful commercial application of MES, a deep understanding of microbial metabolic investigations, bioengineering, electron transfer mechanisms and process optimizing parameters is required to establish MES technology for CO 2 sequestration and value-added products/chemical synthesis.

FULL CONTENT:

Recent advances in microbial electrosynthesis system: Metabolic investigation and process optimization
======================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Abdullah Al-Mamun a, Waqar Ahmed a b, Tahereh Jafary a c, Jagdeep Kumar Nayak a, Ali Al-Nuaimi a, Ahmad Sana a

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bej.2023.108928](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bej.2023.108928 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1369703X23001237&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •Recent advances in microbial electrosynthesis system for metabolic investigation. 
*   •Summarized and analyzed the process optimization parameters in MES. 
*   •CO 2 sequestration and value-added products synthesis by bioelectricity. 
*   •Diversified application of electrogenic microbes and metabolic pathways. 

Abstract
--------

The intensified burning of fossil fuels and the discharging of industrial wastes are severe threats to the environment. The released CO 2 and organic fractions of industrial and municipal wastes exacerbate global warming. Converting the released CO 2 and organic wastes into beneficial electricity and biofuel-chemicals is deemed an environmental necessity. Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) presents a promising technology for bio-electrochemical conversion of released CO 2 and organic wastes into electricity and biofuel-chemicals using external-powered and/or self-powered microbial oxidation/reduction processes. The MES system consists of anodic and cathodic processes. The technology mostly relies on the capacity of electron transfer from electroactive biofilm to the electrode for reducing organics into value-added chemicals and sustaining their respiration and growth. The current review aims to summarize and explore the diversified application of electrogenic microbes and their metabolic pathways of electron transfer. It also summarizes the MES reactor design and operational parameters that influence the catalysis of biofilm and hence, the system performance. The review concludes with a critical evaluation of technical challenges that should be overcome before large-scale implementation. Furthermore, various recommendations on technical perspectives for successful implementation and application, including future research directions, are presented in this study.

Introduction
------------

The synthesis of bio-fuels and value-added chemicals from biomass feedstocks (e.g., sludge, sediments), organic wastes (e.g., lignocellulose, food processing and refinery wastes) and livestock manure (e.g., cattle, poultry farms) has been commenced worldwide at both laboratory and pilot scales [1], [2]. These organic wastes are believed as an important alternative to fossil fuel derivatives [3], [4]. The organic fractions could be converted into energy and bio-fuel chemicals through a sustainable and eco-friendly bio-electrochemical system (BES) approach [5], [6]. Generally, the BES is comprised of an anode and cathode chambers separated by an ion-exchange membrane (IEM). However, some BES reactors were also studied without separators [7]. The BES utilizes electro-active (EA) biofilms as catalysts to drive electrochemical reactions [8], [9], [10], [11]. Such electrochemical conversions consist of oxidation and reduction reactions at anode and cathode, respectively, where the electrodes with the biofilm catalysts are connected to an external circuit [12], [13], [14], [15]. Depending on the operational configuration, BESs comprised of microbial fuel cell (MFC) [11], [16], [17], microbial desalination cell (MDC) [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23], microbial electrolysis cell (MEC) [23], [24] and microbial electrosynthesis (MES) [25], [26]. MFC can harness electrical energy through the oxidation of organic substrates by anodic EA biofilms [5], [27]. Similar to MDC, it can produce electricity as well as achieve remarkable desalination of untreated seawater [22], [28]. In the case of MEC configuration, EA biofilm degraded organics and inorganics using external electrical input to produce bio-hydrogen [29], [30], [31]. These reactors have been extensively studied for various renewable energy/water treatment applications during the last decade. However, all the above BESs face technological and economic limitations for producing bio-energy and bio-hydrogen [32], [33], [34].

MES is a promising technology for energy production through the oxidation of organics on the anode and recovering of value-added chemicals by reducing CO 2 and organics on the cathode. Both the anodic and the cathodic conversions are catalyzed by EA biofilms [32], [33]. Fig. 1 shows possible bio-conversion routes in MES for concurrent sequestration of CO 2, recovery of electrical energy, and value-added chemicals. An important aspect of MES research is to investigate thoroughly the different types of microbes and their mechanisms of accepting the electrons from the electrode to reduce terminal electron acceptors [38]. In the early stages of MES development, mediators improved metal oxidation and the glutamic acid yield from glucose fermentation [35], [36]. In addition, the mediators assisted the methanogenesis in MES reactors that were inoculated by either mixed-culture or pure-culture of _Escherichia coli_, _Klebsiella pneumonia_, and _Zymomonas mobilis_ species [37], [38]. However, it was later discovered that a biofilm of _Geobacter_ species on the cathode could reduce fumarate to succinate, thereby affirming the mediator-free direct electron transfer [43]. Therefore, the investigation of electron transfer mechanisms and the efficient utilization of the revealed electrons in the synthesis of value-added products could improve the performance of MES. In addition, the utilization of mixed-biofilms with diversified metabolism, redox mediators and external e- supply could improve MES performances [32].

It has been reported that various kinds of biodegradable organic-containing streams, especially the discharges from food, beverage, agriculture and refinery industries, were used as electron donors in MES to produce value-added chemicals on the cathode [44], [45], [46], [47]. Studies related to CO 2 utilization as cathodic substrates and its conversion to methane, acetate and other biofuel chemicals have also been extensively reported in the literature [33], [44], [45], [46]. Such conversion of CO 2 into value-added chemicals was considered a promising approach for carbon capture and utilization technology, an important measure of mitigating greenhouse gas emissions [51], [52]. The utilization of CO 2 as substrates in any industrial process might be considered a great blessing for the eco-friendly development of human civilization on earth. Such sequestration of CO 2 was technically feasible due to its abundance in the atmosphere, mainly due to anthropogenic emissions from industrial, agricultural and transportation activities [53]. However, the conversion of CO 2 added several technical disadvantages to the MES system, including (1) significant consumption of electrons for the synthesis of organics and (2) an external supply of energy to activate the autotrophic growth of biofilms [32]. In addition, the imbalance growth of biofilms community at both the cathode and the anode slows the reduction process of CO 2 [49], [50]. To resolve these issues, extensive research is needed to explore the economic, electrochemical and environmental feasibility of MES.

A variety of microorganisms can be used to reduce substrates on the cathode of the MES using CO 2 as the source of carbon. For example, _Chemolihoautotrophic_ microorganisms can be used to reduce inorganic substrates on the cathode to harness their metabolic energy [56]. _Photoautotrophs_ also utilize radiant energy (light energy) for their metabolic activities. Microorganisms, which directly accept electrons from the cathode to reduce substrates, are termed electrotrophs [57]. Studies [35], [58] show that acetogenic bacteria (_Sporomusa spp. and Clostridium spp._) are employed to produce acetate and ethanol by utilizing the gaseous substrates (CO 2, N 2, CO and H 2) on the cathode. The autotrophic bacteria capable of receiving an electron directly from the electrode were also utilized as bio-catalysts during the earlier stage of MES development [55]. It has been found that methane and acetate are the commonly synthesized products during MES operations when using methanogenic and acetogenic cultures, respectively [54], [55]. Forming these desired products requires the selection of suitable microorganisms, which influences the quality and quantity of the end products [48]. During the earlier stage of bio-electrochemical development, pure cultures are utilized for the production of bio-commodities. However, the use of mixed cultures in recent years increased the yield and process performance [58], [59], [60], [61].

The selection of electrode materials impacts the yield and the rate of bio-conversion of chemicals in MES [58], [61]. An ideal electrode material capable of maximizing the performance of MES should have a higher surface area for biofilm development, ease of attachment of biofilms, and high conductivity of biofilm to transfer electrons. An electrode modification by either inserting proper positive charge or fixing the redox molecules on the surface has improved the biofilm-electrode affinity and interaction [62], [63], [64], [65]. The rate of electron transfer between the microorganisms and the electrode surface is normally controlled by reducing the amount of activation energy. The reduction in activation energy was achieved by the application of metal catalysts on the electrode surface [66], [67], [68], [69], [70]. So far, the pathways of microbial electron transfer have been identified by either cytochrome C or nano-pilli or extra-cellular enzyme. However, not all microbes could transfer electrons by all the above three identified pathways. These pathways are very specific for particular microorganisms applied in the MES [40], [70]. Moreover, the electrode with a rough and porous surface could be favorable for developing the electroactive biofilm layers and distributing the substrates [72], [73], [74], [75], [76].

Despite the extensive studies on multiple aspects, the MES technology faces different challenges, i.e., the disproportionate growth rate of anodic and cathodic microbes, cell overpotentials, and energy losses. The previously published review articles mainly summarized the concept of MES, biocathodic production processes, variety of substrates used, the architecture of electrode materials and bioreactor designs, microbial engineering and electron transfer pathways, potential synthesis of value-added products, state-of-art of the MES, and critical review on the recent development of the MES technology (Table 1). However, none of the review articles summarized the parameters for process optimization and types and diversity of microorganisms for effective metabolic investigation. Therefore, to achieve the full potential and successful commercial application of MES, a deep understanding of microbial metabolic investigations, bioengineering, electron transfer mechanisms and process optimizing parameters is required to establish MES technology for CO 2 sequestration and value-added products/chemical synthesis. Therefore, this review article provides recent and comprehensive knowledge on microorganism species, electron transfer routes and operational challenges associated with MES operation.

----------------

Environmental perspective of MES
--------------------------------

MES not only synthesizes biochemicals, but also treats different categories of waste-streams and pollutants, such as discharged streams from fermentation, diluted glycerol and other organic wastes from biodiesel plants. All kinds of produced organic effluents may be converted to value-added chemicals by integrating MES technology [45]. Hence, the integration of MES with the bio-refineries could achieve resource recovery from the waste organics such as oily residues, organic residues, aquatic

Microorganisms involved in the MES process
------------------------------------------

Microorganisms act as biocatalysts in MES reactors to accomplish bio-electrochemical reactions [92]. A variety of microbes for the synthesis of products involving both pure and mixed cultures have been investigated by several studies to evaluate the performance levels of MES [93], [94], [95], [96]. The use of mixed cultures did not require sterilization to facilitate interspecies electron transfer. Therefore, the microbial consortium, usually dominated by acetate and methane-producing species,

Electron transfer mechanisms in bio-electrochemical process
-----------------------------------------------------------

In BES, great varieties of microbes are used to exchange electrons with solid-state electrodes. Thus, microbe-electrode interactions are deemed as one of the key factors for microbial metabolism, which influences the production of chemicals and fuels [128]. However, the electron transfer rate between microbes and electrodes is one of the fundamental criteria for determining the MES performance. Effective parameters on MES performance
---------------------------------------

Generally, the MES cell comprises an anode chamber and a cathode chamber separated by an ion-selective membrane, electrodes to which biofilms are attached, and an external electrical circuit with/without a source of applied potential. Fig. 1 demonstrates the basic concept of MES, showing the routes of substrate circulations with simultaneous recovery of energy and value-added chemicals. Organic substrates are pumped into the cathode chamber with suitable media for biofilm growth. On anode,

Current challenges and future developments
------------------------------------------

The continuous development of MES technology through successive evolution in the selection of reactor designs, materials of construction, type of organisms, appropriate substrates and optimized operating conditions have led this technology to potential conversions of various types of biomass/waste streams into value-added commodities. The MES can represent a sustainable approach as redox reactions for substrate degradation as well as product synthesis can be powered through renewable resources. 

Conclusions
-----------

MES is a promising bioelectrochemical approach for reducing various waste streams into value-added biofuels and biocommodities. This review article thoroughly explains the different microorganism types and their electron transfer mechanisms. Moreover, the article investigates the influence of various design and operational parameters of MES. # Robust and biocompatible catalysts for efficient hydrogen-driven microbial electrosynthesis

[*Communications Chemistry*](/commschem)
**volume 2**, Article number: 45 (2019)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

10k Accesses

110 Citations

8 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

CO2 reduction by combined electro- and bio-catalytic reactions is a promising technology platform for sustainable production of chemicals from CO2 and electricity. While heterogeneous electrocatalysts can reduce CO2 to a variety of organic compounds at relatively high reaction rates, these catalysts have limitations achieving high selectivity for any single product beyond CO. Conversely, microbial CO2 reduction pathways proceed at high selectivity; however, the rates at bio-cathodes using direct electron supply via electricity are commonly limiting. Here we demonstrate the use of non-precious metal cathodes that produce hydrogen *in situ* to support microbial CO2 reduction to C1 and C2 compounds. CoP, MoS2 and NiMo cathodes perform durable hydrogen evolution under biologically relevant conditions, and the integrated system achieves coulombic efficiencies close to 100% without accumulating hydrogen. Moreover, the one-reactor hybrid platform is successfully used for efficient acetate production from electricity and CO2 by microbes previously reported to be inactive in bioelectrochemical systems.

## Introduction

CO2 as a sole carbon source offers an attractive platform for the sustainable production of chemicals and fuels, with the potential to avoid emitting annually 38.2 billion tons of this greenhouse gas while increasing the independence from fossil resources. Renewable energy technologies are providing new and competitive alternatives for production processes based on (bio)electrochemical reduction of CO2 to chemicals[1](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR1 "Aresta, M. Carbon Dioxide as Chemical Feedstock (John Wiley & Sons, Weinheim, Germany 2010)."),[2](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR2 "Harnisch, F., Rosa, L. F., Kracke, F., Virdis, B. & Krömer, J. O. Electrifying white biotechnology: engineering and economic potential of electricity‐driven bio‐production. ChemSusChem 8, 758–766 (2015)."). Assuming that these chemicals can be combusted as fuels, a carbon-neutral fuel and chemical cycle can be envisioned (cf. Fig. [1a](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig1)).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42004-019-0145-0/MediaObjects/42004_2019_145_Fig1_HTML.png)

Process overview. Schematic of a directly integrated bioelectrochemical system for sustainable production of chemicals from electricity and inorganic carbon. **a** Illustration of the envisioned carbon-neutral economy. **b** Integrated bioelectrochemical reactor; anode (+) and cathode (−) compartments are separated by a proton-exchange membrane (CEM). The target reaction on the cathode is the evolution of hydrogen as an electron donor for the microbial reduction of CO2. The displayed microbial catalysts are homoacetogenic bacteria (purple rod) and methanogenic archaea (green cocci) as demonstrated in the presented study. Efficient conversion of electricity and CO2 is achieved for the target products acetate and methane, respectively, via highly biocompatible transition-metal-based cathodes in microbial growth media (Coulombic efficiency near 100%). The system can be powered by any electricity source including renewable sources

A significant advantage of heterogeneous electrocatalysis processes for reducing CO2 to C1 and C2+ compounds are high reaction rates in relatively mild reaction conditions[3](#ref-CR3 "Bushuyev, O. Electrochemical conversion of CO2 to useful chemicals: current status, remaining challenges, and future opportunities. Curr. Opin. Chem. Eng. 2, 191–199 (2013)."). However, selectivity to multi-carbon compounds remains a major challenge, leading to low energy efficiencies and mixed product streams[3](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR3 "Bushuyev, O. S. et al. Cobalt‐nanocrystal‐assembled hollow nanoparticles for electrocatalytic hydrogen generation from neutral‐pH water. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55, 6725–6729 (2016)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Sun, Y. et al. Hydrogen‐mediated electron transfer in hybrid microbial‐inorganic system and application in energy and environment. Energy Technol. 
                    https://doi.org/10.1002/ente.201800987
                    
                   (2019)."). Microbial CO2 reduction reactions, on the other hand, are highly specific, selective, and stable. Moreover, metabolic engineering approaches continuously broaden the product spectrum to include high-value chemicals of industrial relevance[15](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR15 "Dürre, P. & Eikmanns, B. J. C1-carbon sources for chemical and fuel production by microbial gas fermentation. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 35, 63–72 (2015)."),[16](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR16 "Woo, J. E., Song, S. M., Lee, S. Y. & Jang, Y.-S. in Consequences of Microbial Interactions with Hydrocarbons, Oils, and Lipids: Production of Fuels and Chemicals (ed. Sang Yup Lee) 1–16 (Springer International Publishing, Cham, Switzerland 2017)."). Natural electron donors for microbial CO2 reduction processes are H2 and CO and direct supply of electrons for microbial metabolism via a cathode has been demonstrated in microbial electrosynthesis[17](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR17 "Lovley, D. R. & Nevin, K. P. Electrobiocommodities: powering microbial production of fuels and commodity chemicals from carbon dioxide with electricity. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 24, 385–390 (2013)."),[18](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR18 "Kracke, F., Vassilev, I. & Krömer, J. O. Microbial electron transport and energy conservation—the foundation for optimizing bioelectrochemical systems. Front. Microbiol. 6, 575 (2015)."). Several industrially relevant chemicals such as methane, acetic acid, butyric acid, isobutyric acid, hexanoic acid, ethanol, isopropanol, butanol, isobutanol, and hexanol have been shown to be produced via microbial electrosynthesis from CO2 and electricity[19](#ref-CR19 "Cheng, S., Xing, D., Call, D. F. & Logan, B. E. Direct biological conversion of electrical current into methane by electromethanogenesis. Environ. Sci. Technol. 43, 3953–3958 (2009)."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Ganigué, R., Puig, S., Batlle-Vilanova, P., Balaguer, M. D. & Colprim, J. Microbial electrosynthesis of butyrate from carbon dioxide. Chem. Commun. 51, 3235–3238 (2015)."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Nevin, K. P. et al. Electrosynthesis of organic compounds from carbon dioxide is catalyzed by a diversity of acetogenic microorganisms. Appl. Environ. Microbiol. 77, 2882–2886 (2011)."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Vassilev, I. et al. Microbial electrosynthesis of isobutyric, butyric, caproic acids and corresponding alcohols from carbon dioxide. ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 6, 8485–8493 (2018)."),[23](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR23 "Arends, J. B., Patil, S. A., Roume, H. & Rabaey, K. Continuous long-term electricity-driven bioproduction of carboxylates and isopropanol from CO2 with a mixed microbial community. J. CO2 Utilization 20, 141–149 (2017)."). However, these processes currently remain limited by low direct electron uptake rates from the cathode and by a limited number of microbes that are able to directly acquire electrons from solid state electrodes for CO2 reduction[18](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR18 "Kracke, F., Vassilev, I. & Krömer, J. O. Microbial electron transport and energy conservation—the foundation for optimizing bioelectrochemical systems. Front. Microbiol. 6, 575 (2015)."),[24](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR24 "Rabaey, K., Girguis, P. & Nielsen, L. K. Metabolic and practical considerations on microbial electrosynthesis. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 22, 371–377 (2011)."). Thus, an integrated platform that combines the advantages of rapid electrochemical reduction reactions with highly selective microbial syntheses is desirable.

The common electron donor for microbial CO2 reduction is molecular hydrogen, which can be produced electrochemically via the well-established hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). HER is kinetically more facile than electrochemical CO2 reduction and achieves simultaneously high current densities and selectivity[25](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR25 "Zeng, M. & Li, Y. Recent advances in heterogeneous electrocatalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction. J. Mater. Chem. A 3, 14942–14962 (2015)."). Hybrid processes have been proposed that couple the electrocatalytic H2 production in one process to microbial CO2 reduction in a second microbial gas fermentation step for the production of chemicals[26](#ref-CR26 "Haas, T., Krause, R., Weber, R., Demler, M. & Schmid, G. Technical photosynthesis involving CO2 electrolysis and fermentation. Nat. Catal. 1, 32–39 (2018)."),[27](#ref-CR27 "Ramachandriya, K. D. et al. Carbon dioxide conversion to fuels and chemicals using a hybrid green process. Appl. Energy 112, 289–299 (2013)."),[28](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR28 "Torella, J. P. et al. Efficient solar-to-fuels production from a hybrid microbial-water-splitting catalyst system. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 112, 2337–2342 (2015)."). However, such two-step processes entail the pumping and mixing of a low-soluble and explosive gas (H2) at significant costs and safety risks[29](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR29 "Takors, R. et al. Using gas mixtures of CO, CO2 and H2 as microbial substrates: the do’s and don’ts of successful technology transfer from laboratory to production scale. Microb. Bbiotechnol. 11, 606–625 (2018).").

In fact, most bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction platforms seem to be driven by molecular hydrogen as intermediate[30](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR30 "Bajracharya, S. et al. Biotransformation of carbon dioxide in bioelectrochemical systems: state of the art and future prospects. J. Power Sources 356, 256–273 (2017)."). The cathode material of choice in these systems is commonly carbon-based due to its low price and proven biocompatibility. However, production rates in microbial electrosynthesis can be increased significantly by modifying the cathode material towards more efficient HER, e.g., by introducing transition metals, such as nickel[31](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR31 "Zhang, T. et al. Improved cathode materials for microbial electrosynthesis. Energy Environ. Sci. 6, 217–224 (2013)."),[32](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR32 "Nie, H. et al. Improved cathode for high efficient microbial-catalyzed reduction in microbial electrosynthesis cells. Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 15, 14290–14294 (2013)."). And while a direct integration of electro- and microbial-catalysis with H2 as an intermediate offers the possibility to increase overall electron transfer rates, it also presents a significant challenge, because the optimal environmental conditions for both processes differ substantially. While the electrocatalytic HER is best controlled under either strong acidic or alkaline conditions and in ultra-pure electrolytes, microbial synthesis proceeds at circumneutral pH, in the presence of essential media supplements (e.g. trace metals and vitamins) and with high concentrations of CO2. Severe deactivation of inorganic catalysts by microbial growth media components was observed in previous studies[33](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR33 "Gimkiewicz, C., Hegner, R., Gutensohn, M. F., Koch, C. & Harnisch, F. Study of electrochemical reduction of CO2 for future use in secondary microbial electrochemical technologies. ChemSusChem 10, 958–967 (2017).") as well as toxic effects towards the microorganisms caused by dissolution of the cathode material[34](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR34 "Liu, C., Colón, B. C., Ziesack, M., Silver, P. A. & Nocera, D. G. Water splitting–biosynthetic system with CO2 reduction efficiencies exceeding photosynthesis. Science 352, 1210–1213 (2016).").

Due to their high electrochemical stability, transition-metal-based electrode materials were identified as particularly promising, and sustainable alternatives to Pt as catalysts for HER[35](#ref-CR35 "Eftekhari, A. Electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy 42, 11053–11077 (2017)."),[36](#ref-CR36 "Yuan, H. & He, Z. Platinum group metal-free catalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction in microbial electrolysis cells. Chem. Rec. 17, 641–652 (2017)."),[37](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR37 "Vesborg, P. C. & Jaramillo, T. F. Addressing the terawatt challenge: scalability in the supply of chemical elements for renewable energy. RSC Adv. 2, 7933–7947 (2012)."). Here, we select the inexpensive, earth-abundant cobalt-phosphide (CoP), molybdenum-disulfide (MoS2), and nickel–molybdenum alloy (NiMo) as cathode materials as these show particularly promising properties at circumneutral pH[35](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR35 "Eftekhari, A. Electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy 42, 11053–11077 (2017)."),[36](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR36 "Yuan, H. & He, Z. Platinum group metal-free catalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction in microbial electrolysis cells. Chem. Rec. 17, 641–652 (2017)."),[38](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR38 "Miao, J. et al. Hierarchical Ni-Mo-S nanosheets on carbon fiber cloth: a flexible electrode for efficient hydrogen generation in neutral electrolyte. Sci. Adv. 1, e1500259 (2015)."). We test their biocompatibility in a directly integrated bioelectrochemical system for production of chemicals from electricity and CO2 (cf. Fig. [1b](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig1)), and characterize the electrochemical stability of the different materials under biologically relevant conditions. Using methanogenic archaea and homoacetogenic bacteria, we demonstrate stable and high rates of CO2 reduction to methane and acetate, respectively, at coulombic efficiencies of close to 100% and thereby a successfully integrated platform.

## Results

### Biocompatible hydrogen production by non-precious metal cathodes

We first characterized hydrogen production rates of cathodes with CoP, MoS2, and NiMo as HER catalysts in pure bicarbonate buffer as an approximation for a biocompatible electrolyte for microbial CO2 reduction at neutral pH. Platinum was used as the reference catalyst for the HER in all experiments. The catalysts were prepared on planar silicon substrates of identical geometric surface areas. The resulting flat electrode surface enables direct comparison of the biocompatibility of the different materials in the integrated conditions by minimizing any convoluting effects introduced by surface roughness of the catalysts. The here presented rates were integrated over a period of 3 h to emphasize the long-term rather than initial performance.

At neutral pH in CO2-sparged aqueous solutions (0.03 M NaHCO3, pH 7), all three catalysts produced hydrogen at rates in the same order of magnitude as the benchmark catalyst platinum (see Fig. [2a](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig2) and Supplementary Table [1](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1)). At all tested potentials (−0.6 to −1 V vs standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)) NiMo electrodes outperformed Pt, while the performances of the CoP and MoS2 electrodes was constantly lower than that of Pt. This trend became more pronounced with decreasing cathode potential. The highest H2 production rate of 48.0 ± 1.5 µmol h−1 cm−2 was observed for the NiMo alloy at a potential of −1 V vs SHE and a coulombic efficiency of 98.0 ± 3.1%. At the same working electrode potential, platinum achieved an average H2 production rate of 39.0 ± 1.5 µmol h−1 cm−2 at a CE of 99.0 ± 3.8%. The H2 production on CoP and MoS2 cathodes occurred at average rates of 11.1 ± 1.6 µmol h−1 cm−2 and 10.0 ± 1.1 µmol h−1 cm−2 and CEs of 94.1 ± 13.6% and 94.0 ± 9.2%, respectively. It is noteworthy that the HER performance can be significantly impacted by electrochemical conditions such as pH[39](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR39 "Strmcnik, D. et al. Improving the hydrogen oxidation reaction rate by promotion of hydroxyl adsorption. Nat. Chem. 5, 300–306 (2013)."),[40](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR40 "Landers, A. T. et al. The predominance of hydrogen evolution on transition metal sulfides and phosphides under CO2 reduction conditions: an experimental and theoretical study. ACS Energy Lett. 3, 1450–1457 (2018)."), CO2 exposure[40](#ref-CR40 "Landers, A. T. et al. The predominance of hydrogen evolution on transition metal sulfides and phosphides under CO2 reduction conditions: an experimental and theoretical study. ACS Energy Lett. 3, 1450–1457 (2018)."),[41](#ref-CR41 "Cave, E. R. et al. Trends in the catalytic activity of hydrogen evolution during CO2 electroreduction on transition metals. ACS Catal. 8, 3035–3040 (2018)."),[42](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR42 "Zhang, Y.-J., Sethuraman, V., Michalsky, R. & Peterson, A. A. Competition between CO2 reduction and H2 evolution on transition-metal electrocatalysts. ACS Catal. 4, 3742–3748 (2014)."), and buffering anion concentration[43](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR43 "Shinagawa, T. & Takanabe, K. Electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution under densely buffered neutral pH conditions. J. Phys. Chem. C 119, 20453–20458 (2015)."),[44](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR44 "Jeremiasse, A. W., Hamelers, H. V., Kleijn, J. M. & Buisman, C. J. Use of biocompatible buffers to reduce the concentration overpotential for hydrogen evolution. Environ. Sci. Technol. 43, 6882–6887 (2009)."). While a direct comparison to other state-of-the-art catalysts is challenging due to the aforementioned factors, the results clearly indicate that all tested catalysts can achieve favorable hydrogen production rates for biointegration, with the NiMo alloy presenting the most promising material under the conditions tested.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42004-019-0145-0/MediaObjects/42004_2019_145_Fig2_HTML.png)

Hydrogen evolution rates under biocompatible conditions. Hydrogen evolution rates (HER, bar graph, left axis) and corresponding coulombic efficiencies (CE, scatter plot, right axis) of CoP, MoS2, and NiMo cathodes compared to platinum, the benchmark catalyst for H2 production. White bars and circles: platinum; turquoise bars and diamonds: CoP; dark blue bars and triangles: NiMo; purple bars and squares: MoS2. **a** At different potentials in 0.03 M sodium-bicarbonate buffer, pH 7. **b** At constant potential of −0.9 V vs SHE in 0.03 M NaHCO3-buffer and two different microbial growth media. Homoacetogen medium: modified-DSMZ-879; methanogen medium: modified-DSMZ-141. All reported HERs are averaged over a 3-h period. Given values are the mean of biological triplicates with error bars displaying standard deviations. For more details on experimental conditions, refer to the Methods section

Next, the activities of the different electrodes were characterized in the presence of different microbial growth media to evaluate their sensitivity towards required microbial media additions to the electrolyte. When tested in the presence of a yeast extract-containing complex medium used for growth of homoacetogenic bacteria (homoacetogen medium, 1 g L−1 yeast extract) and a marine minimal salt medium (methanogen medium, salinity of 30 g kg−1), cf. Fig. [2b](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig2), hydrogen evolution rates by CoP, MoS2, and NiMo electrodes were not significantly affected, indicating insensitivity of the materials towards the presence of essential growth compounds. In the complex homoacetogen medium, a slightly increased current density (+10%) was observed for both CoP and MoS2 (see Supplementary Table [1](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1)), while the corresponding coulombic efficiencies were slightly reduced from 94.0% and 96.0% to 84.7% and 83.0%, respectively (see Fig. [2b](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig2)). This reduction in electron recovery was likely due to unspecific reduction reactions of organic compounds (from supplemented yeast extract) on the cathode surfaces, which seems to negatively affect the selectivity for H2 of both materials under these conditions. In summary, all three cathode materials exhibited relatively high selectivity for HER and remarkably stable performance under biological relevant conditions.

### Integrated bioelectrochemical synthesis of methane and acetic acid

Next, we investigated the performance of CoP, MoS2, and NiMo electrodes in integrated bioelectrochemical systems for microbial production of acetate and methane from CO2 and electricity. In this proof-of-concept study, 1 cm2 flat cathodes were introduced into H-type, two-chamber reactors that are used for microbial electrochemistry[45](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR45 "Deutzmann, J., Sahin, M. & Spormann, A. Extracellular enzymes facilitate electron uptake in biocorrosion and bioelectrosynthesis. MBio 6, e00496–00415 (2015)."). A constant current of 1 mA cm−2 was applied to the cathodes while CO2 was provided as 20 vol/vol% in N2 gas atmosphere in the reactor headspace. These conditions provide an optimal environment for the selected microbial conversions, therefore excluding a limitation of the overall process by the microbial component.

In one set of experiments, the homoacetogenic bacterium *Sporomusa ovata*, which metabolizes CO2 and H2 to acetate (Eq. ([1](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Equ1))), was introduced as microbial organism to investigate production of acetate as a liquid chemical. In a second set of experiments, we used the methanogenic archaeon *Methanococcus maripaludis* that reduces CO2 with H2 to CH4 (Eq. (2)), to investigate the production of a stable, gaseous hydrocarbon for energy storage.

Electrocatalytically produced H2 was efficiently used by both microorganisms for CO2 reduction to acetate and methane, respectively, at high selectivity (Fig. [3](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig3), Supplementary Fig. 2 and Supplementary Table [2](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1)). Electron recovery in the respective product was near 100% in each case (see Supplementary Table [2](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1)). Figure [3](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#Fig3) shows the production of acetate and methane in the integrated bioelectrochemical system using a NiMo cathode and *S. ovata* and *M. maripaludis*, respectively. The performance of the same system using CoP and MoS2 is summarized in Supplementary Fig. [2](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1) and Table [2](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1). Importantly, no H2 gas was detected (<1 vol/vol%) in the reactor headspace of each experiment throughout the entire duration of 48 h, indicating efficient and stable microbial uptake of the electrochemically produced H2 despite its low solubility in aqueous solutions. In fact, microsensor measurements[46](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR46 "Nielsen, M., Larsen, L. H., Ottosen, L. D. M. & Revsbech, N. P. Hydrogen microsensors with hydrogen sulfide traps. Sens. Actuators B Chem. 215, 1–8 (2015).") of H2 concentration profiles in the bioelectrochemical system revealed elevated H2 concentrations only in close proximity, 50–100 µm, of the cathode surface (cf. Supplementary Fig. [3d](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1)). While the aqueous H2 concentration in abiotic, Ni–Mo cathode-containing reactors was around 220 µmol L−1, the presence and metabolism of *M. maripaludis* reduced that concentration to 0.2–0.6 µmol L−1 (cf. Supplementary Fig. [3a, c](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#MOESM1)). Such efficient transfer of electrons from cathode into the microbial metabolism via hydrogen without the accumulation of high gas concentrations is an important feature of this directly integrated system for two main reasons: first, the use of H2 as intermediate circumvents the requirement of microbial electrosynthesis for a direct electron transfer and, therefore, extends the spectrum of microbial production hosts to possibly any gas fermenting microorganism. And secondly, the efficient H2 uptake close to the electrode surface could enable increased production rates as it is known that production rates in gas fermentation are commonly hydrogen limited[15](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR15 "Dürre, P. & Eikmanns, B. J. C1-carbon sources for chemical and fuel production by microbial gas fermentation. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 35, 63–72 (2015)."),[47](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR47 "Yasin, M. et al. Microbial synthesis gas utilization and ways to resolve kinetic and mass-transfer limitations. Bioresour. Technol. 177, 361–374 (2015)."). Future research aiming to balance and maximize rates between inorganic HER and microbial CO2 reduction will facilitate engineering efforts to further advancing the technology[29](/articles/s42004-019-0145-0#ref-CR29 "Takors, R. et al. Using gas mixtures of CO, CO2 and H2 as microbial substrates: the do’s and don’ts of successful technology transfer from laboratory to production scale. Microb. Bbiotechnol. 11, 606–625 (2018).").

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42004-019-0145-0/MediaObjects/42004_2019_145_Fig3_HTML.png)

Bioelectrochemical production of acetate and methane. Performance of integrated bioelectrochemical systems for acetate (**a**) or methane (**b**) formation from CO2 and electricity, consisting of the two model organisms *Sporomusa ovata* and *Methanococcus maripaludis* and the in situ H2-producing cathode NiMo. Electrons were supplied with a constant current density of 1 mA cm−2 and total electrode surface was 1 cm2. The products acetate and methane are plotted as electron equivalents [mmol]. Dotted line: electrons; triangles: hydrogen; yellow diamonds: acetate; red circles: methane; blue triangles in stacked plots: cathode potential vs SHE. Given values are the me

enriched_points.expanded_content: Microbial electrosynthesis utilizes diverse microorganisms as biocatalysts to facilitate bioelectrochemical reactions, enhancing efficiency in converting substrates into valuable products.

Electrochemical reactions in microbial electrosynthesis involve anodic oxidation and cathodic reduction, driven by applied electrical power, facilitating the transformation of waste into useful chemicals.
Microbial electrosynthesis effectively reduces CO₂ through microbial metabolism, converting it into organic compounds like acetate and methane, contributing to carbon capture and utilization efforts.
This process results in the production of various organic compounds, including biofuels and chemicals, which are valuable for sustainable energy solutions and environmental remediation.
